data.title: Barings/MassMutual's Credit Expertise: The 'Intel Inside' All 4 Barings Funds
data.description:
Barings/MassMutual: Private Credit's "Pros from Dover"

I first wrote about Barings Corporate Investors Fund (MCI) about five years ago (link here), explaining how its sponsor/manager, MassMutual, had been among the top "private placement" investors for many years. "Private placements" was the term originally used for privately issued corporate debt (i.e., loans and bonds not originated by banks), which was a staple of the institutional investment market (insurance companies, pension funds, endowments, etc.) for decades, long before it became more available to retail investors and popularly known as "private credit."

The expression "Pros from Dover" was originated in the 1970 film M*A*S*H by characters Hawkeye and Trapper, two zany Korean War army surgeons who were as likely to use the phrase to talk their way into hospital emergency rooms as they were to crash exclusive golf clubs. The "Pros from Dover" term stuck and is frequently used to describe those with widely recognized, demonstrated expertise, like Barings/MassMutual.

MCI and its almost identical sibling fund, Barings Participation Investors (MPV), have definitely shown themselves, over many years, to be the "Pros from Dover" in the high-yield corporate credit world.

While included as "high-yield bond" funds in most closed-end fund listings, both MPV and MCI emphasize in their reports and presentations their broad experience in, and focus on, the "private credit" space. Here's how they express it:

MCI and MPV benefit from being part of the larger Barings North American Private Finance (“NAPF”) platform, which has over 30 years of experience and commitments of over $28 billion to private credit.

The NAPF platform has provided two primary benefits: Direct deal origination and credit underwriting. NAPF has served as the Lead or Co-Lead on over 80% of its originated transactions and has had a senior loan loss rate of 0.03% since inception. The benefit of being the Lead or Co-Lead lender is the ability to lead negotiations on terms and exercise influence over the credit agreement.

MCI and MPV continue to benefit from NAPF’s strong origination relationships with private equity sponsors. Every private placement investment in the funds' portfolios was directly originated by Barings via a sponsor without a financial intermediary, which means one hundred percent of the economics are passed through to investors.

MCI's Total Returns:

MCI was started back in 1971. Here is its total return, both measured on market price and on net asset value ("NAV") over the past 15 years. On its NAV (which is how traditional open-end funds, like those sold by Vanguard or Fidelity, are measured), MCI was #2 of 24 funds, #1 of 21, and #1 of 17 for the past 5-, 10-, and 15-year periods. On a market price basis, MCI was #2 of 24, #4 of 21, and #2 of 17 for the same periods.

(If hard to read, click on the chart, and it expands and becomes clearer; otherwise, open it here: MCI_TR_new.png)

[Note that Total Return equals the sum of the cash distribution a fund pays during the reporting period, plus the change in the fund's value from the beginning to the end of the reporting period. "Total Return NAV" measures that change in value in the fund's Net Asset Value (i.e., the underlying total value of all the assets that the fund holds). "Total Return Market Price" measures that change in value based on the market price of the fund's shares during the reporting period, not the underlying value of the assets in the fund itself. If the Total Return NAV is greater than the Total Return Market Price, it means that the fund's price discount must have increased and/or its premium shrunk. In both cases, it means the market gave the fund less than full credit for the performance of its underlying assets.

If the Total Return Market Price is greater than the Total Return NAV, it means the fund's premium must have increased and/or its discount shrunk. That means the market increased the price of the fund proportionately more than the performance of its underlying assets actually warranted.

Most long-term investors regard the Total Return NAV to be the more meaningful of the two, since it tells the real story of how much the fund management has actually increased or decreased the underlying value of the fund's asset during the reporting period. Most economists and market experts (including some Nobel Prize winners) believe that the market will ultimately adjust a fund's or any other asset's price to reflect its actual economic value (i.e., the amount of cash income it generates over its life), as this article (link here) explains.]

MPV's Total Returns:

Competing with MCI for those top rankings has been its younger sibling MPV, started in 1988, with the same management team and virtually the same portfolio. MPV's record, below, shows it as #1 of 24 funds, #2 of 21, and #2 of 17 for the past 5, 10, and 15 year periods, with total return measured on an NAV basis. On a market value basis, MPV was #1 of 24, #1 of 21, and #1 of 17 for those periods.

(If hard to read, click on the chart, and it expands and becomes clearer; otherwise, open it here: MPV_TR_NEW.png)

Similar... But Different !?!?

It's no surprise that MPV and MCI have been neck-in-neck for leadership of the high-yield closed-end fund sector over the years, because, as mentioned earlier, the two funds are almost identical, even though one (MPV) is about 17 years younger. Same management and almost identical portfolios. To illustrate this, here are the lists of the top 10 holdings of each fund:

MCI is on the left, and MPV is on the right. Not that it makes much difference, because nine of the 10 top holdings are identical.

"But if that's all true, and the funds are so similar," some sharp-eyed reader is probably saying, "then why are their total returns so different in some key time periods?"

It's a good question and goes to the heart of why discounts and premiums are so important in the closed-end fund market. Here are the premium changes for MCI and MPV over the past year:

If we take a look at the total return tables for MCI and MPV above, we note that the one-year performance, measured on NAV, of both funds was identical: 6.9% each for MCI and MPV, respectively. Yet when the accountants add in the change in each fund's market price in relation to its underlying NAV total return (i.e., calculate its total return on market price), we see that for the same one-year period, MPV had a robust total return on market price of 10.8%, i.e., almost 4% higher than its NAV total return.

Meanwhile, less popular sibling MCI, with the same underlying performance at NAV (a positive 6.9%), didn't even come close to breaking even in total return on market price, with a negative -9.4%, i.e., over 16% worse than its underlying NAV total return. Comparing the total return on market price for the two funds - MCI and MPV - we see more than a 20% difference for essentially the same underlying performance. What gives?

The difference is easy to explain. The market, for whatever reason, decided to increase MPV's premium from about 10% a year ago to currently about 17%. This extra 7% price premium, over and above the underlying performance of the assets inside the fund, explains why MPV's performance - in market price terms - is so much higher than its performance in terms of its NAV. (Closed-end fund net asset values are often approximations, especially for less liquid asset classes, so the numbers won't necessarily match exactly. But directionally, it clearly explains what is going on.)

Meanwhile, its sibling MCI had the opposite experience, in spades. Its underlying NAV total return performance, as we have seen, was a not terrific but respectable 6.9% for the past year (just like MPV's). Yet its price went from a premium of 23% a year ago to a recent reported premium of only 6%, a negative 17% drop. That -17% would account for the difference in total return of -16.3% between MCI's positive one-year total return on NAV of 6.9% and its negative total return on market price of -9.4%.

Why Did This Happen?

The closed-end fund market, in general, can be pretty idiosyncratic and not always totally rational. But my best guess is that the answer lies in MPV's and MCI's respective premiums a year ago. We just said that MCI's premium 12 months ago was about 23%. Now it's 6%. Meanwhile, MPV's premium was about 10%, and now it's up over 17%. Given the similarity in the funds, 12 months ago MCI's premium was about twice that of MPV's, so investors obviously saw an opportunity to choose the cheaper one, MPV, over MCI, either swapping out of MCI and into MPV and/or investing new money into MPV. Both are pretty thinly traded funds, so it would not have taken a huge volume of trading to move their respective premiums, "rewarding" MPV with a better total return on market price than its underlying performance on NAV actually "deserved;" while "punishing" MCI with a lower total return on market price than its underlying performance "deserved."

Now the funds have swapped relative positions from a year ago. MCI had been at a premium more than twice that of MPV's. Now MPV's premium is way more than twice that of MCI's. So are we likely to see a reversal of the switch in investor preference we saw a year ago? Time will tell, but that would be my expectation.

Both funds have quirky distribution schedules, as we pointed out in an earlier article, and historically have tended to delay announcing and paying their "first quarter" dividend until after their annual meeting in early May. This means their four quarterly dividends end up all being paid between June and early January. This leaves about a five-month gap that confuses investors new to the funds as well as closed-end fund data providers, who often list the funds as "not paying a dividend" during the period just before the annual meeting. (Check it out here on Seeking Alpha.) So, we don't know if any further pricing surprises await us before or after the annual meeting (and expected dividend announcement) scheduled for May 14.

Given how virtually identical the two funds are, MCI is clearly the better buy today and would have to be rated a "Buy" to MPV's "Hold," for investors trying to decide which to reinvest distributions in or put new money into and wanting to make the better-value choice between the two funds.

Additional Options

But before we rush out to buy more MCI, there may be some additional and possibly better current choices to consider for those of us who are fans of Barings/MassMutual and have a high regard for its deep bench and long experience in the corporate credit arena, but also consider both MCI and MPV too expensive at their current premiums.

The Barings North American Private Finance (“NAPF”) platform, which we mentioned earlier, services the entire Barings/MassMutual lineup of funds and other investment vehicles when it comes to private credit, secured lending, and structured credit. The lineup includes two in particular that are familiar to many of us and that we hold in our Inside the Income Factory Core Model portfolios:

  1. Barings Global Short Duration High Yield Fund (BGH) is managed by Sean Feeley, who is one of the members of the MCI and MPV management team. There's also additional crossover at both the portfolio management and executive/director levels. BGH has a fine record of performance, with almost as high rankings as its siblings for total return over the years: #5 of 24 for 1-year total return on market price, #4 of 24 for 5 years, #3 of 24 for 10 years, etc. Here is its overall record:

(If hard to read, click on the chart, and it expands and becomes clearer; otherwise, open it here: BGH_TR_new11.png)

In summary, BGH offers:

  • Same top-quality management as MCI and MPV
  • Solid performance record
  • 10.4%+ distribution rate (higher than MCI or MPV)
  • -4.9% price discount

While I have held and written about MPV and MCI personally for quite some time, I have currently switched my personal holdings and Core model portfolio holdings to BGH because of the better value, yield, and discount.

2. Barings Business Development Company (BBDC) is a business development company (BDC) that Barings took over in 2018 (previously Triangle Capital) and then added several other mergers to the mix (Sierra and MVC) to create what is the current BBDC, which has by now been totally integrated into the Barings credit platform. I find it a very attractive way to benefit from the Barings/MassMutual expertise and experience while also enjoying the higher yield (11.6%) and generous discount (-19%) that so many well-managed BDCs are offering these days because of the overly negative media attention that "private credit" and the BDC industry (that specializes in private credit) have attracted over much of the past year.

Here is BBDC's performance record:

(If hard to read, click on the chart, and it expands and becomes clearer; otherwise, open it here: BBDC_TR_newmmm.png)

BBDC's performance has really improved in recent years as some of its legacy (pre-Barings) assets have been worked out and Barings/MassMutual has brought its long-standing expertise to bear. Note that its total return on market price has been #4 of 48 for the past year and #7 of 43 for the past three years. The -19% price discount provides quite a cushion against any potential surprises, and the 11.8% dividend yield is 100% covered by net investment income. I also hold BBDC in my personal portfolio, as well as in our Core model portfolios, in addition to BGH, as mentioned above.

Bottom Line

The Barings/MassMutual credit platform is one of the best in the business. Traditional funds MCI and MPV demonstrated this for a long time but have become too expensive, especially with the recent "dueling premiums" situation between the two funds.

Fortunately, there are these two other Barings vehicles - closed-end fund (BGH) and Business Development Company (BBDC) that also have that "Intel Inside" as the computer firms used to say, but this time it's Barings/MassMutual's long history and experienced credit platform that's "inside," while offering very attractive yields, discounts, and prices.

data.title: Rivian: R2 Is Launching At Just The Right Time
data.description:
While the S&P 500 has quickly shed its wartime jitters and rallied back to all-time highs, there are still plenty of pockets in the stock market where caution abounds. Investors have backed away meaningfully from earlier-stage tech companies, limited not just to software names impacted by the "SaaSpocalypse" but the electric vehicle space as well.

Shares of Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), the second-largest EV maker behind Tesla, have seen their share price fall ~10% this year, and the stock is also down ~20% below December peaks around $22. Meanwhile, the growth backdrop for this stock remains unassailable as we prepare for the launch of the R2 in a few months.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Rivian (RIVN) in November, when the stock was trading at just $13 per share. In retrospect, I wish I had loaded up on more Rivian stock. At the same time, I'm comfortable holding this stock through the R2 launch and am confident in further tailwinds ahead. I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating for the company.

There are two major catalysts ahead for Rivian. The first is the company's upcoming Q1 earnings release, due on April 30. The second is the start of R2 pre-orders, which are expected to open in June. Stay long ahead of then.

Q1 Look-Ahead: Pay Attention To ASP Growth And Software Sales

As a reminder, the earnings releases for most EV companies are a less volatile event because companies pre-release delivery figures about a month before releasing financials. For Q1, Rivian delivered 10,365 vehicles and affirmed its earlier guidance calling for 62k-67k in deliveries in 2026 (inclusive of new R2 figures and representing a rapid 42%-59% y/y growth rate against FY25 deliveries of 42.2k). For reference, delivery trends in the past year are shown in the chart below:

For Q1 specifically, deliveries of 10.4k well exceeded expectations for just 9.9k deliveries in the quarter. We also find the fact that Rivian grew deliveries at 20% y/y versus 8.6k in Q1'25 to be exceptional after a well-known product transition.

Even though vehicle deliveries are already a known data point, there are several core indicators that investors should watch out for in Q1 results:

  • ASP trends. We're hoping for Rivian's automotive revenue growth to exceed 20% y/y growth in deliveries. This would indicate that average selling prices of Rivian vehicles rose in Q1. What this would signify to me is that the deliveries strength in Q1 is coming from higher-end models of R1T and R1S, who are unlikely buyers of R2.
  • Software revenue. Another core indicator is Rivian's trend in software and services revenue. In Q4, software revenue of $447 million grew at 108% y/y, now representing roughly one-third of the company's overall revenue. We note that Rivian has an innate advantage here: though Rivian vehicles are positioned as premium luxury rides, its Autonomy+ software is priced substantially lower at $50/month or $2,500 for an outright perpetual purchase. Tesla (TSLA), meanwhile, charges $100/month for its FSD (full self-driving) package and eliminated the prior option to make a one-time purchase for $8,000. The sharp growth in software revenue indicates strong attach rates for Autonomy+ that are also beginning to turn into a competitive advantage for Rivian.
  • Further commercialization of self-driving technology. Earlier in April, on the heels of Rivian's deal with Volkswagen, Rivian's CEO, RJ Scaringe, also noted his intention to approach more traditional carmakers for an opportunity to license Rivian's software. Adding more OEM customers for Rivian software would be a major, capital-light tailwind to the company's gross margin development.

While we don't expect Q1 to make major waves for Rivian stock, it should be a largely positive catalyst as we wait for the R2 Performance launch in June.

R2 Is Entering The Market With New Tailwinds And Favorable Competitive Dynamics

Broadly speaking, we don't expect Rivian to make any changes to its guidance for the year, which calls for 62k-67k vehicle deliveries in 2026, ahead of any demand data for R2. The R2 lineup is expected to follow a staggered release cadence, with the high-trim $58k R2 Performance launching in June. This will be followed by the $54k R2 Premium in late 2026, the $48.5k R2 Standard Long Range in early 2027, and finally the $45k R2 Standard in late 2027.

But in my view, the stage is set for Rivian to outperform its guidance and potentially raise its forecast in Q2. To me, the competitive landscape has shifted in Rivian's favor. Last year, many potential car buyers were still holding out hope that Tesla would fulfill its promise of a lower-cost entry vehicle priced around $25,000.

What we got, instead, are the 2026 Model 3 at $36,990 and the 2026 Model Y at $39,990. These were modest price drops that were more than offset by the loss of the $7,500 federal EV tax credit that expired in September 2025. These new 2026 Model 3/Y variants extinguished hopes that a cheaper Tesla model is on the horizon. Many Rivian bulls had feared that the R2 launch would be coming on the heels of a cheaper Tesla reveal, but this turned out not to be the case.

The price gap between Rivian and Tesla shrinks further when we consider customers who want full self-driving features.

Consider, for example, a $58k R2 Performance vehicle. Paying $50/month for Autonomy+ over a 5-year ownership period results in ~$61k in total cost before taxes and fees. Compare that to the $45k Premium trim (or similarly priced $57.5k Performance trim) for Model Y; adding 5 years of Tesla FSD at $100/month is an additional $6k in gross cost.

We note as well that all Rivian purchases come with a longer 2-month trial of Autonomy+, versus just 30 days of Tesla FSD at purchase. Rivian's software pricing, set at half of Tesla's and with an additional perpetual purchase option that is ~70% lower than Tesla's now-extinct $8,000 option, is helping the company to shrink its price gap to Tesla. Compares will get even better as we move into 2027 and the <$50k variants of R2 are launched.

Vis-à-vis gas vehicles, we note that the ~30% increase in gas prices since the Iran conflict began has made many more car buyers open to purchasing an electric vehicle, providing further tailwinds for Rivian that may drive a boost to its deliveries guidance later this year.

Risks And Key Takeaways

Of course, there are also risks on the horizon for Rivian. The company could bungle the R2 launch and be plagued with negative reviews and feedback. Also, Rivian's gross margins are still razor-thin; we don't expect a substantial upward correction in the company's gross margin profile until it is selling R2 vehicles at a substantial scale.

Stay long here and hold onto this stock ahead of the R2 launch.

data.title: Trulieve Cannabis: Preparing For Rescheduling
data.description:
Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (TCNNF) is again soaring with the cannabis sector on signs the Trump Administration is moving forward with rescheduling cannabis. Ironically, the stock is back to where the sector traded back in early October when cannabis stocks jumped on more promises of a move by President Trump. My investment thesis remains Neutral on the stock, preferring for investors to Buy dips versus chasing big rallies.

Regulatory Uncertainty

The cannabis stocks are soaring on signs the Trump Administration plans to move forward with rescheduling cannabis from Level I of the Controlled Substances Act (CSA) to Level III. Trulieve Cannabis jumped nearly 20% on the news, but the initial media reports appear to mis-categorize the path forward.

According to Marijuana Moment reporting, the DEA plans to announce another administrative hearing to start the process of the reclassification. This is a multi-month process that got derailed under President Biden, not something that finalizes the rescheduling this week.

The reclassification move would strip the 280E tax requirement from plant-touching cannabis businesses and provide potential for the government to pardon past 280E tax debt. For now, the details are too limited to know the full financial impact.

On the Q4'25 earnings call, CEO Kim Rivers outlined her view on the financial benefits of the government reclassifying cannabis as follows:

... it removes the punitive tax burden of 280E, lifting pressure on state legal operators and allowing conversion from the illicit market to regulated channels. Finally, rescheduling sets the stage for much needed reform such as safer banking and eventual uplisting to U.S. exchanges.

Trulieve Cannabis expects to report Q1'26 earnings on May 7. The multi-state operator (MSO) operates 234 retail dispensaries and over four million square feet of cultivation and processing capacity in the United States, mostly focused on the Florida market.

The MSO reported 2025 revenues flat with the prior year at $1.18 billion with strong adjusted EBITDA of $427 million and a 36% margin. Trulieve Cannabis recorded 2025 cash flow from operations of $273 million and free cash flow of $229 million.

The big question with the likes of Trulieve Cannabis is the unknown tax implications, with the company recording a $668 million liability for uncertain tax position liabilities.

As a reminder, the 280E tax requirement disallows cannabis operators from deducting operating expenses from federal income tax. The companies are left with a large tax bill, in some cases more than the income before tax profits produced by the business.

Trulieve cannabis has been one of the more aggressive MSOs in tackling the unfair tax position. The company has challenged the tax code and filed amended federal tax returns claiming refunds.

Path Higher

Trulieve Cannabis is in a solid financial picture before taking into account any tax issues. The company has a cash balance of $256 million with debt at $232 million with a 9.4% interest rate.

The MSO forecasts a tough Q1 with revenues down at least low single digits. The consensus analyst estimates have revenues falling 4.5% to $285 million.

The key to the financial story is that Trulieve Cannabis is producing strong cash flows with a target of $250 million in 2026 and free cash flow of at least $165 million, though the numbers are down from last year. The MSO will spend more on capex this year due to opportunities in Georgia and Texas.

The stock has 196.5 million fully diluted shares outstanding with a market cap of just $1.6 billion. Trulieve Cannabis only trades at roughly 4x adjusted EBITDA targets around $400 million.

Trulieve Cannabis should definitely rally from here on the removal of the 280E tax burden. The big question is how much of the unpaid tax liabilities the company would end up owing and whether the reclassification actually occurs.

Just as with the rips in October and December on President Trump's Executive Order, the stock is best bought and owned on dips. The stock market likely gives back most of these gains once the DEA becomes fully involved in the reclassification process again and investors face the uncertainty of the regulatory body officially making the move.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Trulieve Cannabis is attractively valued outside of the uncertainty of tax liabilities. The stock likely slips back to the $6 level, where Trulieve Cannabis becomes a lot more appealing on the next cannabis reclassification rip, possibly one tied to the actual regulatory move by the DEA.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Stifel Financial: IB Leads The Way Despite March Dealmaking Snag
data.description:
Stifel Financial Corp. (SF) logged in a good quarter. They have countercyclical elements in their trading volume-based commission businesses, and their IB business is not of massive scale and therefore is capable of scrappily winning share and keeping an idiosyncratic growth rate. While there are still a lot of questions around the Iran War, we think that some of this strong performance should be able to continue in the currently opened books. We do expect sponsor markets and tech to be a weaker dealmaking area for advisory shops in the US, but this shouldn't matter too much to SF, which is indexed to typical mid-market categories like industrials, as well as some healthcare and depositories, though we have some concerns around those areas in the mid-market as well. In terms of business composition and profile, SF is quite similar to Raymond James (RJF). So are their valuations. Therefore, there isn't really a value angle here. While dealmaking is supposed to be recovering already from a March slump, we are a little concerned about the relative performance of the mid-market.

Results

SF logged a decent quarter. In the lending business, there's very little exposure to software, hence nothing related to the SaaS-pocalypse to worry about. Similarly, SaaS-exposed BDC and private credit also don't have much bearing on the SF business. Results were as follows:

There was 22.3% YoY growth in revenues. Transactional revenue, being a less vigorous growth area in general, was a drag on that figure. Their transactional revenues have a meaningful countercyclical or at least a pro-volatility component where the KPI here is levels of activity. Volatility in the later March period would have brought that about and lent a helping hand to revenues from this segment as markets clearly repositioned to a new geopolitical moment and a new situation for supply chains, still rather unresolved. Equity and bond markets seem to be shrugging off the latest headlines, with peace talks still uncertain though the ceasefire is prolonged for the moment. Volatility is coming down, so a surge in activity should decelerate developments here for the moment but could also come back in force if things go the opposite way of peace in the Iran War.

On the other hand, a calmer environment for markets will be helpful for the asset management division. The recovery in broad indices too should help grow the base for management fees. AM was already a lift up on the overall revenue growth this quarter, and acceleration should be visible. AUMs did stall among some AM players as of March, so we expect some relief.

For the IB business, which drove revenues, we have more ample commentary. Both capital raising and advisory did well. Capital raising would have been penalized by the volatility. This is a counter-volatility business for sure, with IPOs and issuances shelved from day to day depending on market performance and waiting on developments. We expect some deferrals, as issuers will likely want more definitive answers on the Iran War before proceeding. For advisory and other types of dealmaking, the excellent growth is in contrast to an observed fall in dealmaking activity towards the end of March, as bad as on Liberation Day last year. SF had bucked this trend, obviously, with the mid-market exposure, less leverage to high debt, high profile, large ticket, and capital cost-sensitive deals coming in clutch. The US mid-market - and SF is heavily indexed to the US over the rest of the world - is concerned significantly with industrials, healthcare, and depository institutions. Depository institutions and healthcare (including biotech, which has an exacerbating reflexivity issue) are cost-of-capital sensitive, so we expect some amount of pressure regardless of the reported recovery in dealmaking activity in the currently open quarter, though perhaps SF's emerging scale can buck that trend.

Specifically on the depository side, there’s a lot of uncertainty, and this uncertainty is impacting buyers. You read the press saying about $150 oil and interest rates may be rising and what happens to credit spreads, et cetera. I think that there’s a pause.

Q1 Earnings, RJ Kruszewski - CEO of SF

Cost of capital is at issue because of the reinflation concerns around the War. But the US focus probably protects them from acute supply issues of oil, where the US has the benefit of being self-sufficient. In terms of any sponsor exposure, geopolitical concerns and reinflation concerns that drive leverage could limit the vigorousness of their deferred return to market, so we don't expect that to be a major delta for results. Still, a generally accommodating dealmaking environment, particularly in key areas like industrials, consumer goods, and healthcare, and an idiosyncratic profile should permit more growth, and equity markets calmed in April should accommodate not just M&A but also capital-raising deals.

For interest-driven businesses, the upward translation of the yield curve could be an opportunity to transform funding sources into better-yielding loans, which they do retail and commercially through Stifel Bancorp. Interest on idle cash likewise can be used to squeeze a bit better yields out of the upward translation of the yield curve.

Bottom Line

It's a bit of a challenging environment. Some verticals are not liking the cost of capital questions, to be sure, including depositories and reflexive companies like biotech within the healthcare vertical. But on the latter point and for capital raising in general, markets are shrugging things off for now, though we aren't sure how long that can last if the Iran War doesn't settle down as is being expected by markets based on their rise. The idiosyncratic SF mid-market profile should be able to generate growth regardless, though, we'd expect. However, on valuation, we don't see much of an angle. The IB exposure in overall revenues is similar to RJF. Otherwise, they similarly have AM, sales and trading, and other financial advisory activities, including a bank. They are a fair comp in our view, and both trade just above 13x. So no relative value angle. Though assuming more growth that can outpace comp and generate operating leverage and profit growth, there may be an absolute case. 13x isn't much given growth. Still, we aren't heavily compelled.

data.title: Tesla: Don't Fall For The Hype, It's A Sell
data.description:
Article Thesis

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) reported its fiscal Q1 earnings results on Wednesday, beating estimates on both lines, which was a positive surprise. Still, Tesla continues to battle with relatively weak profitability levels, which is why I think that shares aren't attractive at the currently very elevated valuation.

Past Coverage

I have written about Tesla here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in January, when I published this article. I was bearish then, which has worked out well so far — since that article was published, TSLA is down by double digits, while the broad market (SPY) delivered a positive return over the same time frame. With TSLA reporting its Q1 results on Wednesday, I want to update my views on the company — I'm somewhat less bearish today but remain bearish overall, as I'll lay out in this article.

What Happened?

Tesla reported its Q1 results on Wednesday, following the market's close, with the headline numbers from the report looking like this:

Tesla delivered a double beat, as it did during the previous quarter, which naturally is good news — both its sales and its profits were higher than what Wall Street analysts were expecting. The immediate market reaction was positive, with TSLA trading up by a couple of percentage points at the time of writing. Let's delve into the report.

Tesla's Q1: Not As Bad As Feared

Starting with Tesla's revenues, we see that the company's sales were considerably higher compared to one year earlier, with a 16% year-over-year increase. Of course, this is still way below Tesla's longer-term guidance, and it is also important to note that the comparison was relatively easy, as Q1 2025 was an especially bad quarter for the company. When we compare the Q1 2026 revenue result to Q2, Q3, and Q4 of 2025, we see that it wasn't a great quarter — the average revenue over the previous three quarters was a little above $25 billion, so TSLA was below that in Q1. Still, the year-over-year performance was positive, and that's a good thing.

Revenue growth was driven by higher sales for both Tesla's automobile business and its services business, with year-over-year growth for these two units coming in at 16% and 42%, respectively. Tesla's energy business, however, generated lower revenues compared to one year earlier, showing a 12% decline — I don't think this is necessarily a reason to worry, though, as this business tends to be lumpy. Neither especially high relative growth rates nor a decline during a single quarter are very meaningful in the long run — project timing etc., can result in some swings on a quarterly basis.

Looking at the performance of the automobile business, production was up nicely versus one year earlier, but this is partially due to retooling in Q1 2025. Deliveries didn't grow much, with a 6% year-over-year increase across TSLA's entire model portfolio, which isn't a great result considering the comparison was far from tough. There's some good news in that, though — automobile revenues were up by 16%, while deliveries were up by only 6%, which means that average prices were up considerably. Since TSLA had been battling with average selling price pressures and weak margins for some time, a significant increase in Tesla's vehicle prices was good news and helped the company improve its margins, as we will see later.

Tesla produced around 50,000 more vehicles than it sold during the first quarter, which made its inventory levels rise considerably, ending the quarter at 27 days of sales versus a low of just 10 days of sales a couple of quarters earlier. This trend should be watched — if inventory levels continue to climb, Tesla will eventually have to either reduce production, which would mean higher per-vehicle fixed costs, or it will have to reduce prices to move more vehicles. If that happens, the margin improvement we saw in Q1 may vanish again. It is also possible that Tesla, as well as its peers, benefits from the current energy market turmoil — high gas prices may result in higher EV sales this year, which could help Tesla in dealing with its growing inventory.

Tesla's services business showed the best relative revenue growth rate, partially driven by higher FSD demand. While FSD is still only self-driving under supervision, the number of active subscriptions has risen by around 50% over the last year — I believe this is a pretty good, some may argue a surprisingly good, growth rate. If Tesla is able to deliver nice FSD subscription growth in Q2 and beyond, Tesla's services business should remain a growth driver for the foreseeable future.

On a company-wide basis, Tesla's gross margin improved very nicely, rising from 16.3% to 21.1%. Higher average selling prices and higher utilization in the automobile business, and the growth seen in Tesla's services business, explain this positive development. When we look at adjusted margins, where one-time items are backed out, the improvement was less drastic, but TSLA's adjusted EBITDA margin still expanded by a nice 180 basis points over the last year, although Q1 2026 was weaker than Q4 2025.

The cash flow picture wasn't great, with Tesla generating around $6 billion in operating cash flow and around $1.5 billion in free cash flow. Annualized, that's $6 billion. Tesla is thus not at risk of running out of money at all, but a $6 billion free cash flow run rate translates into a free cash flow yield of just 0.4% for someone buying into TSLA today — that doesn't seem attractive to me.

Is Tesla A Good Investment?

On the bottom line, Tesla reported profits of $480 million — up 17% from one year earlier, but still pretty weak considering Tesla is valued at around $1.5 trillion today. When we annualize the Q1 profit, we get to around $2 billion in profit, which would translate into an earnings multiple in the 700x to 800x range. Tesla's adjusted numbers look better, although one can argue whether backing out items such as share-based compensation makes sense — due to the dilution it causes, it still is a real cost for shareholders. But even when we take the Q1 adjusted net profit at face value, at $1.45 billion, Tesla's valuation remains very elevated, as the Q1 earnings run rate would mean a ~250x earnings multiple. Tesla has shown some growth in important metrics in Q1, selling more vehicles at higher prices and adding new subscriptions to FSD, but it is by far no Nvidia (NVDA) — which grew its revenues by more than 70% in the most recent quarter. If I can buy Nvidia and its gigantic growth at 24x net earnings, why would I want to buy Tesla at 250x earnings?

To me, it doesn't seem that the current business is worth $1+ trillion, so that makes an investment in Tesla a bit of a bet on unproven businesses like robotics. It is possible that Tesla ultimately has a lot of success there, but that's far from guaranteed — many other players are active in this space as well, and we also don't know where margins will stabilize for the robotics industry in the future. Some investors may decide that paying 250x adjusted net earnings (or 750x GAAP net earnings) for a company like Tesla is a good idea due to its potential in emerging business areas, but I don't want to take this bet. We saw some improvement in Q1 versus previous quarters, but there were also negatives, such as the big inventory build-up.

I think TSLA stock looks somewhat better today compared to a quarter ago, but it still looks considerably overvalued to me, so I remain bearish for now and think that there is more downward risk than upside potential in the foreseeable future, despite the positive after-hours reaction.

data.title: Bally's: Balance Sheet Needs To Sustain Growth Ambition
data.description:
Bally's Corporation (BALY) still has significant growth ambition. The gaming operator's majority-owned Bally’s Intralot S.A. (IRLTF) is in talks with Evoke plc (EIHDF) to expand more into the European gaming market. Ultimately, Bally’s current very high debt, existing investment plans, and mixed financials don’t seem to support constant new acquisitions—I believe that Bally’s remains a very risky investment.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my previous September 2025 article on the stock, titled “Bally's: High Debt, High Ambition in Las Vegas.” The stock has since returned 4%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has returned 6%.

High Debt Makes Bally's Evoke Acquisition Ambition Risky

Bally’s growth ambition remains very high. After completing the previous transaction with Intralot in late 2025, creating Bally’s Intralot, of which Bally’s holds a controlling 58% stake, the company has already announced it will acquire a Louisiana casino from Boyd Gaming Corporation (BYD). Meanwhile, it is eyeing expensive multi-billion investments in new casinos in both New York and Las Vegas. Bally's is trying to grow in many directions.

The mentioned growth investments don’t describe Bally’s ambition completely; Bally’s Intralot is now also in talks to acquire Evoke and has made a $304 million offer to acquire the company. Evoke is a European gaming operator with notable brands like William Hill, 888casino, Mr. Green, and others, generating $2.4 billion in revenue and $175 million in operating income over the past twelve months.

The acquisition target has nearly $2.4 billion in interest-bearing debts as well, though. Evoke’s equity valuation has been pushed very low as the company is struggling to manage its debt load.

In my opinion, the transaction’s rationale is shaky. It’s uncertain whether Bally’s Intralot and Evoke would have significant operational synergies, which would make the acquisition target more valuable under Bally’s management. The acquisition is proposed to be made with an all-share combination, causing noteworthy dilution to Bally’s Intralot’s share count. More notably, Bally’s Intralot would have to take on a massive amount of additional debt in the combination.

Bally’s, or Bally’s Intralot, of which the company owns a majority stake, doesn't seem to be in a position to take on much further leverage. Inclusive of consolidated Bally’s Intralot debt, Bally’s already has more than $4.5 billion in total interest-bearing liabilities and needs to finance a casino acquisition from Boyd as well as massive casino investments in New York and Las Vegas. Bally’s recently already announced a $700 million real estate sale-and-leaseback alongside a credit facility agreement; the company has had to sell assets to fund its growth ambition.

Ultimately, no definitive agreement has been signed yet to acquire Evoke. Current talks may end without a deal. I believe that unless clear synergies are shown in the transaction, such a deal is very risky for Bally’s Intralot and Bally’s as a majority shareholder due to added debt.

Bally’s Financial Performance Has Been Mixed

It’s important to also focus on Bally’s current earnings. Full-year 2025 pro forma results show a mixed performance across Bally’s businesses; the main Casinos & Resorts segment reported a -2.7% organic revenue decline to $1527 million and a sharp -9.0% adjusted EBITDAR decline to -$400 million due to increased competitive pressure in a number of markets. Total Bally’s Intralot revenues grew by 2.2% to $909 million due to a great increase in B2B revenues, and the newly formed company’s adjusted EBITDAR improved by 7.4% to $361 million. At the same time, despite great revenue growth in North America Interactive, the segment’s earnings remain slightly negative for Bally’s.

In total, 2025 adjusted EBITDAR declined by -0.4% to $683 million on a pro forma basis, not showing very good momentum.

Going forward, I believe that Bally’s should show better earnings growth in 2026. The company’s permanent Bally’s Chicago location is planned to open to the public during 2026. Already after the company has invested hundreds of millions into the location, Bally’s is acquiring a location from Boyd and opened its new entertainment complex at Bally’s Baton Rouge in December 2025. The previous sale-and-leaseback transaction does weigh on profitability with $56 million in annual cash rent, likely offsetting a good part of momentum from growth investments.

BALY Stock’s Valuation Is Still Volatile

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Bally’s. I now consolidate Bally’s Intralot’s results into Bally’s revenues and EBITDA, but subtract estimated minority interests in the cash flow conversion. I account for the sale-and-leaseback agreement in the model.

The opening in Chicago and Bally’s other growth investments should continue to drive healthy growth in the next few years. I estimate a total 2025-2035 revenue CAGR of 4.2%, ultimately stabilizing at 2% annually. The growth estimates don’t account for investments in New York or Las Vegas, as such investments’ structure and earnings expectations are unknown for now.

Bally’s recent sale-and-leaseback is going to pressure profitability in 2026, but I ultimately expect profitability to stay stable afterwards at a 17.5% adjusted EBITDA margin.

In the cash flow conversion, I expect Bally’s to pay a 25% tax rate on half of EBITDA, that Bally’s Intralot accounts for 60% of total annual EBITDA when calculating minority interests, and that Bally’s will have $120 million in CapEx going forward.

The estimates put Bally’s fair value at $15.4, suggesting a fairly good 29% upside as of writing. Ultimately, despite a good amount of upside in the estimate, I don’t believe Bally’s to be very attractive yet—immensely high debt and forward earnings uncertainty from a number of growth investments make such upside a very fair margin of safety. Small changes in earnings assumptions have a massive impact on the estimated fair value.

The fair value estimate is up slightly from $15.3 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 9.43%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 9.72% interest rate, annualized from Q4 debt and interest expenses. I again expect a very high 200% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For the cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.29% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.18% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the U.S. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.80. With an ESG addon of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.4%, the cost of equity stands at 13.71% and the WACC at 9.43%.

Takeaway

Bally’s rumored acquisition of Evoke seems to be a risky move for the company. Both Bally’s and Evoke have a massive amount of debt, and Bally’s is already eyeing very expensive growth investments in New York and Las Vegas; the balance sheet may not sustain constant debt additions. As the stock remains a very volatile investment but seems to have a fairly balanced valuation, I remain at a Hold rating for BALY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: POET Technologies: The Missing Piece In Marvell's AI Empire
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Previously, in my articles, I referred to POET Technologies (POET) as a "rough diamond" that's gaining value due to the potential acquisition by Marvell Technology (MRVL). Currently, there's indirect confirmation of this thesis, because after the completion of the acquisition of Celestial AI, MRVL is gaining access to key technologies in which the POET team played a direct role. It's my view that POET’s role could become even more significant, shaping it as a player in the global market for chip photonics, the use of which is critical for data centers. The goal of this article is to analyze the role of POET’s technologies for MRVL, reveal its ambitious plans, evaluate the company’s potential value following successful commercialization, and assess the value of the first major $5 million contract. In my estimation, POET has the potential for market capitalization growth of up to $5 billion.

Previous Points

My thesis in previous articles about POET was that it's a "diamond in the rough" that could become a target for acquisition by Marvell. Starting in 2022, the name POET has been repeatedly mentioned in Celestial AI’s financial reports as a technology customer. Subsequently, together with POET, it collaborated on the development of an optical interposer platform, providing cutting-edge solutions for ensuring high computational efficiency in AI infrastructure. Since MRVL announced in December 2025 that it would acquire Celestial AI for $3.25 billion, this technology became available to it. Now that Marvell is transforming into an AI giant, the management and shareholders are interested in utilizing the best technologies used in data centers. Because POET has the technological capabilities to offer specific solutions based on its experience with Celestial AI, it strengthens the company's ties with MRVL. My rating was therefore "Strong Buy," based on my confidence that my predictions will meet optimistic expectations.

POET's Strategy

POET's strategy is built on finding customers to whom it can offer solutions for improving the energy efficiency of computing. The AI supercycle continues due to both the expansion of the GPU fleet and increased energy consumption. Basically, components that have been manufactured on the market to date are not fully adapted to operate at 1.6T speeds. Outdated technologies lead to overheating, an increase in energy consumption, and a decrease in the efficiency of computing operations. Because of this, the data centers are forced to increase their capex on cooling systems. On the other hand, POET offers an optical interposer which eliminates these problems and reduces cooling costs and power consumption.

Blazar is the company's flagship product (formed on a single chip, minimizing the space required for deployment in data centers), providing a ready-made module capable of operating at 1.6Т. The technology is one of the first in the world to combine the advantages of photonics and electronics into a single device, giving the market a unique solution to boost computing efficiency and improve data transmission stability. Meanwhile, many other companies are developing prototypes, but POET has already deployed Semtech’s first 1.6T receiver for practical use. Management has previously stated that the product is currently going through qualification testing on the computing infrastructure of major cloud providers, so a successful test period will enable the company to secure its first major contracts in the future.

The Role of POET Technologies in MRVL’s Ambitions

Today’s excitement surrounding POET shares is largely driven by news of the company’s collaboration with MRVL. POET’s CFO said the company has received an order from MRVL (shipment of products, followed by plans to continue and expand the partnership). Furthermore, additional orders are expected from Foxconn (FXCOF) and Luxshare Precision. These reports do not surprise me in the least, since I'm confident that POET’s technologies can be successfully and reliably integrated into Marvell’s ecosystem. Marvell's custom chips require high-quality photonics, exactly what POET offers. Main expectations that POET's technologies will be crucial for MRVL's ambitions emerged after the $3.25 billion acquisition of Celestial AI was finalized.

It turns out that Celestial AI and POET are in the same business, just in different segments. The former applies photonic technologies in systems that are directly integrated into data centers, whereas the latter develops these technologies to accelerate data transmission. Since 2022, information about POET being a client has repeatedly appeared in Celestial AI’s financial reports. Subsequently, they became partners in a project to jointly develop an optical interposer platform. MRVL acquired Celestial AI specifically to gain access to this technology, and therefore it's impossible to fully realize all of its plans without POET’s participation (as a contractor).

Marvell is currently successfully utilizing the Celestial AI Photonic Fabric in the development of its next-generation custom chips, integrated at 1.6T. As POET is working specifically on a solution for this type of challenge, they're an excellent option for sourcing photonic technologies to incorporate into new products. Naturally are not the only ones in this segment achieving excellent results in designing such technologies. The main competitors, of course, continue to be the market leaders in optical modules: Coherent (COHR) and Lumentum (LITE). For me, though, the main drawback of their business models, based on traditional discrete assembly of optical components, remains significant. Their production is more expensive, making it more difficult to scale than POET’s semiconductor-based method. Their technology is easier and cheaper to scale. It's only a question of POET’s own capabilities, and these still need to be developed and extended, but without major contracts, this is unlikely.

Expectations for the Q1 2026 Earnings Report

Based on Nasdaq estimates, this earnings report is expected to be released on May 13, 2026 (though I, personally, doubt this and expect the report closer to June, as the last report was released on March 31, 2026), for which analysts' consensus forecast anticipates a negative EPS of -0.05% and revenue of $250,000. That said, when we look at the last eight quarterly reports, every single one has delivered a negative surprise in terms of revenue, with EPS coming in below expectations 7 times. That tells us that forecasts aren’t holding up. However, things could change dramatically as early as the next quarterly report. Looking at Fintel's forecasts, they make a bold prediction of $13 million in revenue for POET in 2026, compared to $82 million in 2027.

We should also keep in mind that in November 2025, POET received a $5 million order to supply 800G optical modules. The CFO confirmed that these orders are crucial for expanding production capacity, and by the end of this year, the total order volume will significantly exceed $5 million. The attractiveness of POET for orders will continue to grow due to the growing demand for photonic technologies to address optical interconnect challenges in data centers.

As part of the next quarterly report, shareholders should pay attention to the following metrics:

  • Firstly, there will be a particular focus on finding evidence confirming that the order book is genuine and that the POET platform’s technological leadership is valid.
  • Secondly, it's possible that there will be official comments on how the company plans to implement its strategic move to eliminate its PFIC status, the existence of which creates barriers to share purchases by institutional investors. The relocation of the headquarters to the U.S. will minimize tax risks for U.S. investors, due to which POET is currently undervalued.
  • Finally, investors are keen to hear updates on the company’s collaborations with key partners. Further comments regarding the list of partners and the current status of these collaborations are particularly important. We will likely learn more about the fulfillment of the $5 million order and the scope of the partnership with MRVL—both now and in the future. The current interview with POET’s CFO serves, I believe, in part as a preview of what to expect in the next earnings report.

Valuation of POET

To assess POET’s investment appeal, we cannot use forward values of traditional multiples such as P/E, PEG, EV/sales, and EV/EBITDA. Essentially, the reason for this is the lack of reliable sales and profit figures. For this reason, my intention is to assess the company’s potential value by calculating its market share in the 1.6T segment. Based on Nvidia’s (NVDA) roadmap, in 2026 an average of approximately 6 million Rubin-based GPUs will be produced, using 1.5 transceivers per unit. Therefore, there will be a demand for approximately 9 million 1.6T transceiver ports, each with an average cost of $1,500. Thus, the transceiver market could be worth approximately $13.5 billion in 2026. But POET doesn’t sell finished transceivers; it sells optical engines, and these make up 25% of the total product cost (or $375).

Given that POET is projected to capture up to 10% of the total market share by 2027, sales could reach approximately 0.9 million optical engines. Reasons for this optimistic forecast include the use of semiconductor integration in product assembly, established partnerships with several major cloud providers, and the fact that shipments of 800G products under the first order have already begun. Based on an average product price of $375, sales of POET could reach $337.5 million by 2027.

Using COHR and LITE as examples, they have a median EV/Sales (fwd) multiple of 15.63x. This means that with sales of $337.5 million, a company's market value should be $5.275 billion. Taking into account POET's market capitalization of $1.57 billion at the time of writing, potential stock growth could reach 235%. I believe this is a realistic estimate looking ahead to the end of 2027, taking into account how highly volatile such assets are as they're aimed at participating in the AI supercycle. As an example, over the past year, the market value of LITE has increased 15-fold.

POET's Risk of a Disconnect Between Valuation and Actual Commercialization

POET stock is entering overbought territory, driven by investor expectations ahead of the next earnings report. It could become a key architect of the optical nervous system for AI, though it will need to get past the commercialization phase first. POET's main expenses are currently focused on R&D, not surprising since its core activities are still centered on research rather than production. The management plans to ship more than 30,000 modules in 2026, meaning that any delays in production, whether due to defects or issues with finished product output, may reduce investor confidence.

Moreover, this first major order worth $5 million highlights the company’s extreme revenue concentration. This is insufficient to cover its current losses. Markets are starting to price in more ideal execution of all future contracts, giving a premium to POET’s market value. Also, keep in mind that the company has set a tax trap for itself, so if the US relocation gets delayed, investors who bought shares might pull their money out. Therefore, even if the next earnings report doesn’t meet expectations, this could add weight to the arguments made by detractors.

Conclusion

Taking into account that POET shares have risen from $6.90 to the current $12.40 since my last recommendation (while my target was $15), I'm upgrading my rating from "Strong Buy" to "Buy." The sole purpose of this is to capitalize on volatility in order to purchase shares when the price corrects. In my view, this risk of a gap between valuation and POET’s actual commercialization is justified, as the company is only in the early stages of its entry into the global AI economy market. My plan, accordingly, will be to initiate new purchases of POET during market phases when the price corrects by 10–20%. From a long-term perspective, my opinion remains that the company is poised for commercial success. My target zone for full profit-taking will therefore be closer to the $25–$30 range.

data.title: VOT Is Struggling In 2026, Consider Core Mid-Cap Growth ETFs
data.description:
Despite the positive impact of rate cuts and investors shift towards mid-caps, Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF (VOT) is struggling to generate impressive returns. Year-to-date, VOT is up 1%, underperforming significantly relative to 13% increase of the S&P MidCap 400 Growth Index. The market trends demonstrate that investors have been neglecting upper-middle-market due to its lofty valuations and lower sensitivity to rate cuts. Moreover, VOT’s soft performance is blamed on its portfolio structure compared to the core mid-cap ETFs. Therefore, I downgrade my rating for VOT to hold from buy. I suggest investors to consider the S&P Mid-Cap 400 Growth Index focused ETFs.

Why Does VOT Struggles Despite Favorable Conditions?

While the mid-cap category benefited from a spree of rate cuts and investors shift from expensive large caps to cheap assets, Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF unperformed compared to both large and core-mid caps. Year-to-date, the ETF generated only 1% in price returns. In the past twelve months, its share price was up 23%. On the other hand, year-to-date, the S&P MidCap 400 Growth Index soared 13.5%, extending twelve months surge to 41%. During the same periods, the S&P 500 growth index soared 3% and 47%, respectively.

VOT’s performance signals that its share price has been struggling to respond to both large and mid-cap rallies. In the past few years, investors concentration on mega caps and higher rates negatively impacted its performance. However, despite rate cuts and shift to mid caps in the past few quarters, investors seem less aggressive towards VOT.

The fund offers exposure to an upper end of the mid-cap category. VOT tracks the performance of the CRSP US Mid Cap Growth Index by using full replication technique. The median market cap of its portfolio hovers around $44 billion.

VOT’s soft performance and lack of investor confidence can be blamed on its lofty valuations. The fund currently trades around 35x its trailing earnings, up significantly from 27x of the core mid-cap category. Its P/E ratio is also higher than 33x of large-cap growth category. Similarly VOT’s price to book and sales ratios are also up from core and large-cap ETFs.

In addition, VOT’s portfolio structure lowers its potential to generate market-beating returns. The fund offers nearly a quarter of its portfolio exposure to industrials sector, one of the top-performing sectors so far in 2026. VOT’s industrials concentration is less than the S&P 400 MidCap Growth Index’s 31%. The fund’s top industrials stocks are Vertiv Holdings Co (VRT) and Howmet Aerospace Inc. (HWM). Its exposure to industrials stocks has been positively contributing to its overall performance.

Furthermore, the information technology sector accounts for 23% of its portfolio weight. In the past few years, the most of the tech-driven uptrends were driven by the largest tech stocks, specifically by the mega caps. Whereas, the upper mid-cap tech stocks struggled to deliver gigantic returns. Year-to-date, VOT’s top tech stocks, including DoorDash, Inc. (DASH) and Datadog, Inc. (DDOG) plunged 18% and 5%. Its several other tech stocks are trading in red so far in 2026.

VOT’s consumer cyclical stocks, including Carvana Co. (CVNA), Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. (CMG) and Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) also underperformed in the past few quarters. The financials sector has also been under pressure due to the the impact of rate cuts on margins.

Whereas, so far in 2026, the health care sector ranks among the laggards. VOT’s top health care stocks, including Edwards Lifesciences Corporation (EW) Alnylam Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ALNY), IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. (IDXX) and Zoetis Inc. (ZTS) delivered negative returns. Year-to-date, Alnylam Pharmaceuticals and IDEXX Laboratories are down nearly 20%. VOT has 10% of its weight in the health care sector. The fund’s portfolio also offers a small exposure towards the real estate, communication services, energy and utilities sectors.

Characteristics and Quant Rating

Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF’s portfolio comprises 132 stocks, with the top 10 holdings accounting for 20% of its entire weight. It is a passively managed, index-focused and cap-weighted fund. VOT’s expense ratio of 0.05% is lower compared to the median of all ETFs of 0.50%. The fund’s assets under management hovers around $16 billion, with the three months average shares trading volume of 255 thousand. Its average shares trading volume is up from median of all ETFs of 41 thousand.

VOT earned a hold rating based on the Seeking Alpha analysis, as its price momentum is softer than peers. Moreover, its standard deviation of 14 is up from than the median of all ETFs. Currently, VOT’s per share price is around $282. A higher per share price can lower investors ability to buy shares in a large quantity.

Consider Core Mid-Cap ETFs for Better Growth Prospects

Vanguard S&P Mid-Cap 400 Growth Index Fund ETF (IVOG), which tracks the performance of the S&P MidCap 400 Growth Index by using full replication technique, offers greater growth prospects. Year to date, IVOG’s share price is up 13%. In the last twelve months, the fund’s price appreciated by 47%. Its share price rally is supported by its exposure to the core mid-cap category, which is benefiting the most from investors shift to mid caps from large caps. The fund’s portfolio median market-cap is in the range $12 billion.

In addition, IVOG’s cheap valuations are adding to investor sentiment. The fund currently trades around 27x its trailing twelve months earnings. This is down from both the S&P 500 growth and VOT’s 33x and 34x. Its price to book of 4.5 is also down considerably from its peers.

IVOG’s portfolio structure is also among the biggest catalyst for its price upside, given its greater concentration in the industrials sector, accounting for 31% of its entire weight. Year-to-date, the S&P MidCap 400 Industrials index is up 16%.

Unlike upper-mid-cap tech stocks, the S&P MidCap 400 technology sector is up 28% year-to-date. On the negative side, despite the rate cuts, the S&P MidCap 400 Health Care Index struggled to generate solid returns. Whereas, the utilities, energy, real estate and consumer defensive sectors have been performing well due to low rate conditions and cyclical tailwinds. IVOG earned a buy rating based on the Seeking Alpha quant analysis.

In Conclusion

As the mid-cap category is flourishing due to the rate cuts and investors shift to cheap securities, selecting the right investment is crucial to capitalize on uptrend. Vanguard Mid-Cap Growth Index Fund ETF is likely to underperform compared to the core mid-cap category due to its lofty valuations and large number of underperforming stocks. Therefore, it might not be a compelling mid-cap vehicle to chase the potential uptrend. Whereas, the core mid-cap investment vehicles, such as Vanguard S&P Mid-Cap 400 Growth Index Fund ETF, may enable investors to capitalize on favorable conditions. Its portfolio structure, cheap valuations and solid share price momentum positions it to capitalize on investors shift to mid caps.

data.title: Tesla: A Very Noisy Q1
data.description:
After the bell on Wednesday, we received first-quarter results from Tesla, Inc. (TSLA). The electric vehicle giant got off to a slow start in 2026, as vehicle deliveries and energy deployments both missed Street estimates. The company ended up beating on the top and bottom lines, but the headline numbers may not be as good as they initially suggest. Thus, management will have to walk a fine line on the call for this initial reaction rally to hold.

Previous Coverage of the Name

I last covered Tesla at the beginning of this month, right after the company released production and delivery figures. While vehicle sales rose more than 21,000 units over the prior year period, Q1 2025 was significantly hampered by factory shutdowns for the Model Y upgrade. Energy storage deployments were well below street estimates, so we've seen analyst estimates tumble a bit in the past three weeks. Since that time, Tesla shares have rallied nearly 10%, mostly taking advantage of an 8% rise in the S&P 500 as U.S./Iran war fears have calmed a bit.

A Look at the Q1 Results

Going into Wednesday's report, Tesla analysts were expecting revenues of $22.20 billion, or about 14.8% growth over the prior-year period. Adjusted earnings per share were forecast to surge almost 29% to $0.35 a share. While vehicle sales were hurt by the expiration of certain EV incentives in late 2025, Tesla stepped up some of its promotional activity earlier this year to drive sales in certain markets. In the graphic below, you can see how Tesla fared against my usual three cases. Dollar values and share counts are in millions, except for earnings per share figures.

The company came in a little ahead of the Seeking Alpha average estimate for $22.20 billion in revenues. I was a bit pessimistic this time around, mostly due to the company's launch of cheaper variants and promotions, but Tesla says it actually recorded higher selling prices and had a nearly $1 billion positive revenue impact over Q1 2025 due to currencies. This seemingly says that the company's sales mix shifted towards more expensive vehicles, while full self-driving sales also increased as the one-time purchase of that feature ended during Q1. Energy revenues were quite weak in the quarter, while services revenues jumped a bit.

Total automotive gross margins, including credit sales, increased by nearly 0.7% from Q4 levels, while energy margins rocketed higher by roughly 11 percentage points. However, management noted in the shareholder letter that it benefited from one-time items related to tariffs for both segments, as well as a one-time benefit in the automotive space due to warranties. There was no figure given for the size of the benefit, but based on that energy margin percentage, it seemingly was quite large. Operating spending did ramp up, and the company recorded some large other expenses without providing details. The 41-cent non-GAAP profit beat the street by 6 cents, but it's hard to determine the quality of that beat until we get more details on some of these non-recurring items.

Balance Sheet and Guidance Update

Tesla produced free cash flow of more than $1.44 billion in Q1. However, it only detailed about $2.5 billion in capex, a very small portion of the at least $20 billion it planned to spend for the full year. That means cash flow numbers for the rest of 2026 will be hampered a bit. Total cash rose by about $700 million to over $44.7 billion, while debt and finance leases increased by more than $850 million to over $9.2 billion. During Q1, a little more than $2 billion was spent on an investment in Elon Musk's AI company, X AI, which is now part of SpaceX (SPACE).

In terms of guidance, there weren't any major updates. Vehicle production will continue based on-demand levels, with volume production of the Semi and Cybercab starting this year. First-generation product lines are being installed for volume production of Optimus robots. There are 3 cities in Texas with unsupervised self-driving taxis currently, although 2 launched in recent days in very limited numbers. In addition to the Bay Area with safety drivers in its taxi service vehicles, Tesla plans near-term expansion to five more major cities.

The Current Valuation Picture

When you look at Tesla, the stock itself looks extremely expensive for an automotive company. The stock finished Wednesday at nearly 191 times its expected adjusted earnings for 2026. As a comparison, the big two traditional U.S. automakers' average numbers are in the single digits times their respective expected earnings. Even if you factor in Tesla as a part-tech company, the valuation is still many times that of the major U.S. tech giants, which mostly currently go for 20 to 30 times their respective annual earnings.

In a different light, in the chart below, you can see how Tesla matches up on a price-to-expected sales basis for 2026 vs. other EV names: Lucid Group, Inc. (LCID), Rivian Automotive, Inc. (RIVN), and VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS), XPeng Inc. (XPEV), Polestar Automotive Holding UK PLC (PSNY), BYD Company Limited (BYDDF), and NIO Inc. (NIO). All valuations are as of Wednesday's close.

The average of the seven comparison names was 1.37 times their expected revenues for next year. A number of these other companies are projected to see much higher revenue growth rates than Tesla in the short term. Of course, they are all working off much smaller base numbers. Additionally, Tesla is the only one of these names that has delivered any meaningful amount of profitability and cash flow at times, outside of BYD, but the premium that shares trade for is massive.

As of Wednesday's close, the average price target on the Street was about $416, up about $5 since my previous article. That current average represents more than 7% upside from the day's finish. As you might expect with a stock that has long had such a bull/bear debate, the price target range goes from $125 to $600. There are 48 analysts who have covered the name in the last three months; of them, 23 had some kind of Buy rating, with 18 Holds, and 7 sells.

Final Thoughts and Recommendation

In the end, Tesla announced a very noisy quarter. Expectations went into Wednesday very low after the bad delivery and energy numbers announced a few weeks ago. Thus, Tesla ended up beating very low estimates, although there were some major one-time items that seemingly drove those beats. The balance sheet remains in solid shape, although there looks to be a massive amount of capex spending left for 2026 that could reduce financial flexibility.

TSLA shares jumped initially in the after-hours, but the next few weeks will be dependent on how some of the company's futuristic plans pan out. For the moment, I'm going to keep a sell rating on the stock, just because the valuation doesn't fit the current story. Elon Musk has been hyping things like full self-driving for more than a decade now, and the service remains well behind schedule still. Investors may start to focus more on humanoid robots soon, but until some of these efforts finally bear significant fruit, Tesla remains a very risky name to invest in.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Oracle Of OMAH Wins Round One Vs. Berkshire Hathaway
data.description:
The Solactive VistaShares Berkshire Select Index became operational in 2016 and includes 21 equities. The 21 are comprised of the 20 largest valuation holdings of Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B), along with BRK.B itself.

On the rebalancing dates (February, May, August, and November), 10% of the index is assigned to BRK.B, with the balance of 90% being divided up among the balance 20 securities. Additionally,

...stocks with a weight above 4.5%, including BERKSHIRE HATHAWAY INC-CL B, would collectively have a maximum allocation of 45%. In addition to that, individual stocks are also capped at 20%.

Source: Index Guideline

This index is used for its equity selection by VistaShares Target 15 Berkshire Select Income ETF (OMAH), an actively managed fund that began operations a little over a year ago.

The Fund

OMAH has a two-fold investment objective, with long-term capital appreciation being secondary to earning income. It even has a quantifiable target for its annual income - 15%. Just like the objective, the investment strategy is also two-fold, both parts tied to BRK.B and its holdings (emphasis ours).

1) investing in a portfolio of equity securities based on the Solactive VistaShares Berkshire Select Index ...; and (2) generating income through an options portfolio ...

Source: OMAH Summary Prospectus

The equity portion is managed by the fund sub-advisor, VistaShares LLC, whereas the options portion is managed by the fund advisor. OMAH's equity portfolio serves to achieve its long-term capital appreciation objective. Since OMAH is actively managed, the selections and weightings are not necessarily identical to the index. VistaShares LLC chooses index incumbents based on whether they will help achieve OMAH's objectives. Typically, large-capitalization companies have low volatility, and the index goes with BRK.B's largest 20. Low volatility limits capital gain potential, so at times, OMAH goes shopping outside the index, provided that the chosen ones are still BRK.B holdings (just not in the index). If OMAH steps outside the index, it holds between 21 and 30 equities, which could be a mix of large- and mid-capitalization companies. As of April 17, OMAH held 21 equities, with the top 10 holdings making up close to 62% of the portfolio.

Apple Inc. (AAPL) led the pack, followed by BRK.B, American Express Company (AXP), Occidental Petroleum Corporation (OXY), and The Coca-Cola Company (KO) rounding out the top 5. OXY makes us nostalgic about one of our "oldie-but-goldie" pieces on this platform from 2019. BRK.B had just entered the OXY scene by financing an OXY acquisition. Anyway, coming back to our protagonist du jour...

OMAH writes options or sells calls on its underlying equities. The premiums from the options portfolio are the main source of OMAH's 15% annual income objective. Having said that, while choosing the option strikes, the advisor also keeps the capital appreciation goal in mind. The options strategy is based on the performance of the underlying holdings.

The Adviser employs different options strategies based on its market outlook, selecting one or a combination of strategies it believes will achieve the Fund’s income target, while also allowing for potential capital appreciation

Source: OMAH Summary Prospectus

Option premiums are heavily dependent on the volatility of the underlying equities, or Vega. That is also factored in when choosing the covered call derivatives for OMAH. A closer look shows that all the options (only a portion are shown below, but we checked) expire fairly soon. As we type this on April 21, 2026, you can see there are many expiring on April 24.

So these are not even the monthly but the weekly options. There are, of course, the regular monthly options, and you can see those for Visa Inc. (V), Chubb Limited (CB), and VeriSign Inc. (VRSN). All in all, the option strategy is similar to most option funds that emphasize shorter-term option selling to get premium rather than enhance defensiveness of the strategy.

Typically, the fund invests around 80% of the net assets in the aforementioned index incumbents or option contracts that provide exposure to the index incumbents. OMAH can also have up to 10% of its net assets in cash or Treasuries.

Performance and Expenses

OMAH has annual expenses of 0.95%, comprising entirely of management fees.

Only a year into operations, OMAH has outperformed BRK.B by close to 15% - thanks to it achieving its annual income target. The option premiums have come to the rescue of the fund.

Verdict

OMAH distributes monthly and currently yields 14.70% (market price $18.52).

Investors should know that it is not always guaranteed that the fund will generate actual income equivalent to the distribution rate. This is especially true when funds sell shorter-term options, which can create heavy whipsaws. The main reason to consider investing is if you want something like BRK.B, but with more income. But it is still apples and oranges. With BRK.B you get a certain portfolio that includes these companies and also lots of unlisted securities like Burlington Northern Santa Fe. You get BRK.B's insurance portfolio, and above all else, you get the cash.

Many on this website are used to phrases like "cash is trash," but in our world, it has an optionality. BRK.B can swoop in on assets at distressed prices, and neither the company nor its founder cares what you think about the cost of sitting idle. The options are also likely to create whipsaws that, of course, run contrary to BRK.B's ultra-long-term thinking. So we would bet that the fund and BRK.B, perform rather differently over the long term. But for those who are in favor of piggybacking on the BRK.B holdings with some option income, this might be a good choice. At least they won't be chasing the tech-heavy indices loaded with garbage LLM investments.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it and sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.
